lesson.title: Vector Spaces & Subspaces
lesson.body: A vector space is a set V with addition and scalar multiplication satisfying 8 axioms including commutativity, associativity, existence of zero vector, and distributive laws. A subspace H ⊆ V must satisfy three criteria: contain the zero vector, be closed under addition, and be closed under scalar multiplication. The span of a set of vectors is the set of all linear combinations, and vectors are linearly independent if no vector can be written as a combination of the others.

rooms.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the 8 axioms of a vector space?
steps: Recall: closure, commutativity, associativity, identity, inverse, scalar identity, distributive laws
answers: All vectors have length 1
There exists a zero vector 0 such that v + 0 = v
u + v = v + u for all u, v in V
c(u + v) = cu + cv for all scalars c
rooms.explanation: Having unit length is not an axiom of a vector space. The real axioms include commutativity, zero vector existence, and the distributive laws.

rooms.question: Which of the following subsets of R² is a subspace?
steps: Check: does H contain \mathbf{0}? Is H closed under addition and scalar mult?
answers: The set of all (x, y) with y = 2x
The set of all (x, y) with y = 2x + 1
The set of all (x, y) with x ≥ 0
The set of all (x, y) with x² + y² = 1
rooms.explanation: y = 2x passes through the origin and is closed under addition and scalar multiplication — it's a subspace (a line through the origin). The others fail at least one criterion.

rooms.question: Are the vectors v₁ = (1, 2, 3) and v₂ = (2, 4, 6) linearly independent?
steps: Check: does c_1\mathbf{v}_1 + c_2\mathbf{v}_2 = \mathbf{0} force c_1 = c_2 = 0?
answers: No, v₂ = 2v₁ so they are linearly dependent
Yes, they point in different directions
Yes, both vectors are non-zero
No, because they are in R³
rooms.explanation: v₂ = 2v₁, so 2v₁ - v₂ = 0, giving a nontrivial linear combination equal to zero. They are linearly dependent.

rooms.question: The span of a single nonzero vector v in R³ is geometrically:
steps: All scalar multiples of v form a line through the origin.
answers: A line through the origin
A plane through the origin
All of R³
The zero vector only
rooms.explanation: span{v} = {cv : c ∈ R} is the set of all scalar multiples of v, which forms a line through the origin in R³.

rooms.question: Order the steps to verify that H = {(x, y, z) : x + y + z = 0} is a subspace of R³:
items: Check closure under addition: if (a,b,c) and (d,e,f) are in H, then (a+d)+(b+e)+(c+f)=0 ✓
Check closure under scalar mult: k(a+b+c) = k·0 = 0 ✓
rooms.explanation: Subspace verification requires checking all three criteria in order: zero vector, closure under addition, closure under scalar multiplication.

boss.name: The Axiom Guardian
boss.taunt: Name all eight axioms or face infinite-dimensional oblivion!

questions.question: Which condition alone is sufficient to conclude that a subset H of a vector space V is a subspace?
answers: H is nonempty and closed under linear combinations
H contains at least two distinct vectors
H is a finite set
H contains the zero vector only
questions.explanation: If H is nonempty and closed under the operation cu + v for all scalars c, it is closed under both scalar multiplication and addition, making it a subspace.

questions.question: Is the set W = {(x, y) : xy = 0} a subspace of R²?
answers: No, it is not closed under addition
Yes, it contains the zero vector
Yes, it is closed under scalar multiplication
No, it does not contain the zero vector
questions.explanation: W consists of the x-axis and y-axis. Take (1,0) and (0,1): both are in W but their sum (1,1) satisfies 1·1 ≠ 0, so (1,1) ∉ W. Not closed under addition.

questions.question: Three vectors in R³ are linearly dependent if and only if:
answers: There exist scalars c₁, c₂, c₃ (not all zero) making their linear combination equal to 0
All three vectors are nonzero
The vectors span R³
No vector equals the zero vector
questions.explanation: Linear dependence means there exists a nontrivial (not all-zero) combination of the vectors that equals the zero vector.
